topic: A decision framework across data types
scope: Routing the reader (ties to Appendix A).
key_concepts: No single feature engineering strategy works universally. The decision depends heavily on the data modality, dataset size, and interpretability requirements.
For tabular data, the focus is on scaling, cardinality reduction, and interpretable derived features.
For structured temporal and spatial data, the focus shifts to correct windowing, avoiding leakage, and capturing structural context.
For unstructured text, image, or audio data, the approach leans heavily toward learned representations (embeddings) and pretrained models.
The decision framework routes the practitioner to the correct mental model before writing any code.

definitions.meaning: A structured mapping of data types and project constraints to the most appropriate feature engineering strategies.

examples: If working on a financial fraud detection system with strict regulatory interpretability rules, the framework points towards designed human representations (tabular bins, interaction features) rather than black-box deep tabular embeddings.

relations.Appendix A: Serves as the prose explanation for the technique selection map found in Appendix A.
relations.Ch 3-15: Acts as a capstone routing mechanism across all the data modalities discussed in the book.

exclusions: Do not list every technique from every chapter. Keep it high-level routing.
Do not include the full Appendix A matrix here.
